text: and all the other providers who are integral to making this process work.
I think it's very important to stress that.

Before we even started doing the VATS lobes, we trained our team because the procedure, though less invasive for the patient, is really in some ways a more risky patient in that the chest isn't wide open and it's actually a more difficult procedure for surgeons than doing it with a big open incision.
So one of the most important things we did was to set up a team and do a lot of drills before we even started getting everybody up to speed as to
how we dealt with different situations.
And we have a very dedicated team that scrubs the majority of these cases, which is both a tech and a thoracic nurse, as well as thoracic anesthesia.
Here we're seeing the beginning of the surgical portion where we already have a port with a camera inside the chest space.
Dr. Weiglin, our fellow, Dr. Nam are making an incision.
You see our scrub tech, Tara, who's there with them.
As we're entering into the chest space, they're using electrocautery to make sure that there are no small bleeding vessels from this area.
Through these incisions, we'll be inserting
long, thin tools, as you see there.
Many of the tools are very similar to the instruments we use in open surgery.
However, because of the minimally invasive approach, they have to be somewhat longer and somewhat thinner.

You can see up in the upper corner of the screen the small retraction device, something called a wheat lantern, that's actually just spreading some of the soft tissue up at the top of the screen, as I mentioned.
And though that retractor is in place, we don't use retractors that spread the ribs.
And that's, again, the important part of thoracoscopic surgery.
And the importance of that is simply that it's the rib spreading that actually causes the pain, not the skin incision or separation of the fat.
And the tools that Jim's alluding to there are two very large, almost look like Q-tips, that are very soft dissecting instruments that are used to spread the tissue.
But they're approximately five millimeters in size, just to give you a frame of reference, which is about a fifth of an inch.
It's easy to infiltrate small incisions with long-acting local anesthetic
do intercostal nerve blocks and the patients wake up much more comfortable than after a large thoracotomy.
the instruments that we use like the staplers that help diminish air leak.
So here we're again looking at the inside of the chest cavity.
We're looking at the fissure, the division between the lobes of the lung.
How important is that division between the lobes during this kind of surgery?
It's not critical, but in this particular case, the lobes are very nicely divided and it makes the procedure a little bit easier.
In between those lobes, as you can see there, are some lymph nodes, and those are dissected with the specimen as well.
And underneath that, or between those two lobes, is the artery that feeds the lower lobe and the branches that go to the upper lobe, and that's part of where our dissection will begin.
Here we jumped a little bit back to the front of the heart, and the heart is on the left of your screen.
The vein draining the upper lobe is right there next to that little metal grasper, and that's the left upper lobe in the top right part of the screen.
And what we're doing here is simply starting the dissection.
It's done identical, open, i.e.
with a big incision versus when we do it with the scopes.

We're going to isolate the artery and the vein and the airway or the bronchus that go to the upper lobe to enable us to take it out in one piece to do the same lobectomy that we would do as if we did it with a big open incision.
And now as we look at these structures and some of these instruments, the instruments seem so big.
These instruments are five millimeters in size, which is about a fifth of an inch.
So they're actually very long, but they're not very big.
That's the beauty of this procedure is that...
The camera gives you a nice magnification and you can manipulate that magnification.
You can zoom in and zoom out and see the larger picture and you can see finer structures and I think that would allow surgeons to be so precise in dissections.
In fact, what you're seeing there, although to the non-surgeon may seem like a significant amount of blood, that's about a teaspoon of blood and so it's not a significant amount.
It's just so better visualized with the scope and the light.
When I started doing these, some of the surgeons said, how can you work to such a small hole you can't see?
And it's absolutely not true.
In fact, you see much, much better with the scope than when you did with open surgery.

And one of the benefits of that is everybody in the operating room, from anesthesia to your tech to your nurse, all see what's going on, and they know where we are with the case, and it helps them follow and assist better in the procedure as well.
I'm fostering that team approach.
Here we're going around a blood vessel again in the fissure.
We see again the extended fancy Q-tip that we're using for that soft blunt dissection.
This is just an artery to the upper lobe and this is an instrument that I'm using now.
It's an instrument that I've been using for 25 years open.
It's the identical instrument.
We put it through that slightly larger incision that's about an inch and a half, two inches at the top.

of the screen earlier in the broadcast and what we're doing here is just isolating the artery, getting ready to divide it with a stapler the same way we did it open for years before we converted to the thoracoscopic technique.
So really the operation at the end of the day or even during the procedure

if one didn't know they were looking in the chest, if one didn't realize they were looking at a screen would be identical and I think that's the main goal in doing this operation is to do the identical cancer operation because the first priority is doing the right cancer operation for the patient and the benefit is one the better visibility and the less pain but the importance of doing the right cancer operation and a thorough cancer operation is definitely the priority in any operation.
And now one thing I think that it would be good to discuss is the structure that we're dealing with there and the importance of that structure in this operation.
So the darker area below the vein, maybe you'd like to talk about that lymph node and lymph nodes generally and how important they are in this operation.
Well what we're doing here is gradually developing and dividing the pulmonary arteries to this upper lobe.
The left upper lobe has a variable blood supply and it can have as few as two or even three pulmonary arteries.
This patient had I believe five
small branches to her upper lobe.
And what we're doing is carefully preserving the blood supply to the lower lobe as we individually divide the upper lobe branches and we're reflecting the lymph nodes.
You can see one on the upper lobe specimen right there.

That grabber is actually holding the upper lobe and there's lymph nodes on the lung tissue there that are going to be taken or given to the pathologist with the specimen to ensure that we adequately and accurately stage the patient appropriately.
And what we're doing here is continuing to take additional branches of the pulmonary artery to the left upper lobe.
We're isolating them first and then dividing them in the exact same way we've done them open for many, many years.
I think we have another question from our audience, and that is, is that the normal color of the lung?
And maybe either Dr. Leputkin or Dr. Weigel can answer that, because we all have a role in how that lung looks.
Sure.
Yeah, what we saw in the mock-up is the inflated sort of a large upper lobe.
And here on the screen, we see that this upper lobe is completely collapsed.
And when the lung is, the air exits the lung, the alveoli, those little bubbles that the lung tissue consists of, they kind of stick together and form this kind of a
more solid appearing structure in the chest.
And it's usually more bluish because for one thing we're ligating the vein and it causes a little bit of congestion there.
But when the surgery is done and the rest of the lung that stayed in the chest is being re-expanded, it acquires again normal appearance.
It's pinky, frothy, healthy looking tissue.
Sometimes those black markings in the parenchyma of the lung or in the tissue of the lung is the traces of carbon or particles of sooth or coal that we inhale.
Especially smokers have a lot of them and the lung looks not as pretty as it is here.
So that's a normal situation.
There's nothing wrong with this.
It's just a collapsed lung.
And another question from our viewers, how long does the procedure take?
It depends on the lobe and the patient and the tumor.
The lower lobes tend to be a little bit quicker than the upper lobes because they have less blood vessels going to them.
Here we're continuing to take arterial branches of the upper lobe, soon to be taking bronchus, which you see in the foreground.
As you mentioned, the instrumentation
as we look on the inside in the video, seems very similar to open instruments.
Our trays actually have both thoracoscopic or long skinny instruments as well as our conventional instruments that we're quite comfortable with going around the pulmonary arteries.
And we go back and forth between the instruments, primarily whatever seems to get the angle that we need to get to do the procedure safely.
So again, I think the real emphasis on the instrumentation is that
We really require that our techs in the OR are our dedicated thoracic techs because they know, they're watching the operation and they know what we need before we even ask for them.

It's sort of like having radar assist you and that's really immensely helpful in doing this operation to have a tech who's really in sync, paying attention and watching the operation and knowing what's coming next.
because you really need a team approach in this operation for it to go smoothly and quickly and have a good outcome for the patient.
Two things I would point out just looking at the video here.
One, the piece of thread or the silk tie that is around the vessel I think demonstrates the degree of magnification because that is literally a piece of very fine string.
The other thing that we're seeing here is a stapling device that is being used to divide the vessel.
and control that vessel on either side.
Correct.
For many years now, I would say probably from the early 90s, most of lung surgery, even before the thoracoscopic era, switched to doing a lot of the dissection of these very fragile, fine vessels
with staplers.

A stapler not surprisingly is actually more accurate than individually stitching these arteries closed or tying them because a tie can compress it too tight and divide it just in the motion of tying it.
So the staples are extremely precise and the staplers that we use for the artery is different than the stapler we use for the tissue and then the bronchus.
They're all different thicknesses and they're very carefully precisely developed.
This stapler here is actually
basically was devised specifically for pulmonary arteries.
And it has both the depth of the stapling, the staples themselves, as well as the articulation really facilitate getting around the bends that we sometimes need.
It's not that we always need them, but when we need a particular angle, it's really nice to be able to do that and to prevent having to open the patient, i.e.
make a bigger incision, or put another port just to get the appropriate trajectory.
So stapling has evolved as the procedure has evolved.
Perhaps you could answer, Jim, what percentage nationally of lobectomies are actually done this way now, both by dedicated thoracic surgeons and by general surgeons?
It's a very small minority, actually, probably 25% or so that are being done thoracoscopically when we look at the national numbers.
And that's for thoracic surgeons.
And that's exactly right, which is for thoracic surgeons.
In many instances, the...
the surgeons who are doing the procedures can be general surgeons who have additional training in thoracic surgery and that number would be lower.
So this is certainly not as yet the standard of care for lobectomy but I think
Certainly dedicated general thoracic surgeons are moving in that direction, but it's slower than one would expect.

I think an important point to make to the audience is that the rationale for progressing to this, and there are some that say that it should be the standard of care, although I think that's being debated now, predominantly because there are many surgeons that have not yet

He adapted this technique, but I think that what has been shown, Dr. Scott Swanson has been a pioneer in this, showing that patients who have their lobectomy done thoracoscopically, not only is it safe, but it may be safer with fewer complications.
and also definitely enables patients to get to additional therapy quicker and get through it.

So I think that these are really strong benefits that are potentially going to have it adapted nationally, just like in colon surgery now, laparoscopic colectomy has become really the preferred approach.
I think that we're very close for this becoming and being accepted by the thoracic community as the, quote, preferred approach.
We have another question from an emailer.
Are there patients who want to have VATS lobectomy rather than an open procedure and can't for some reason?
I think like many things it depends on the most important thing is that the surgeon does the operation that he's most comfortable with.
So sometimes there are constraints geographically that surgeons in the area may not have adapted the technology.
I think that in that case patients like any operation should always seek providers that are really experienced in what they do and
I think that it is something that they can and should ask their surgeons and their primary care docs if this technique is available and or where can they have it done.
There are certainly patients that the operation can't be done thoracoscopically or in a minimally invasive way.

And some of our patients who have tumors, primary tumors that are greater than a certain size that we wouldn't be able to take out of our access incision, sometimes the location of a tumor may prohibit us as well.
Well, correct.
I think initially, years ago, we felt that if patients had more advanced disease, if they had nodal involvement, i.e.
if they were stage 2 or stage 3, that was an indication to do it open to make sure that you got all those lymph nodes.
But I think as the technique has evolved in the hands of the people doing this technique for years now,

I think in an experienced VATS surgeon that's not a contraindication and the most important, as we spoke earlier, the most important thing is that all of the appropriate nodes are resected with the specimen and that's really the judgment of the surgeon whether or not he feels or she feels that she can get a complete resection and resect the nodes that are necessary for many patients to be put on trial, certain number of lymph nodes in certain stations have to be resected
Sometimes a larger tumor, if it's more central or abutting the heart, will need to be done in the old-fashioned or more conventional open approach.
But the vast majority of patients, whether they're stage 1 or even up to stage 3A, which means lymph nodes on the same side,
in the areas that you're seeing here near these main blood vessels, they can still be done thoroscopically many, many times.
What we're showing you here, maybe you want to describe technically what we're doing here?
Here we're just seeing the stapler being advanced towards the airway or the bronchus.
Again, you can see the piece of silk thread, basically, that is...
that is being used as a guide around the airway or the bronchus.
This stapling device compresses and lays down three rows of...
of staples on each side and then cuts in between.
So six rows of staples with a cutting device that divides it so that there's three rows on each side.
And this stapler's a different color, correct?
Yeah, a different color, a different size of the staple, and a different amount of compression that we would use for the bronchus as opposed to the vasculature.
And shortly you'll see where we partner with anesthesia, you're going to see the lung on the
lower lobe here, which we're looking at now, basically turn pink and puff up.
And that's Dr. Lepukhinengeng.
What are you doing here, Sergei?
What makes that come back up?
Well, we basically start, we take the clamp off the lumen that was inserted in that particular airway and let the anesthetic mixture get into the lung.
We gently
squeeze the bag that we use for manual ventilation of the lung.
to gently re-expand the alibi.
Make sure that there's no, Dr. Weigel looks at this thing very carefully.
Make sure that there's no air leaks anywhere because we just divided the big airway.
And it looks like the staples in this case work beautifully.
There's no air bubbles coming out of this.
And the... But I think if...
What you could see briefly there, the blue of the big specimen that you're seeing is the deflated lung, and now it's devitalized.
All the blood vessels are divided.

And to the right side of the screen, you could see the pink lung, which was the lung with some gas in it, which you can really see the contrast or the more, quote, normal color here on the right side of the screen.
There's still some compression or collapse on the right side.
So the rest of the lung that stays with the patient
It actually looks normal and the isolated part that's been divided is going to be soon taken out of the chest through a small incision.
What are we putting in there Jim?
We see a large plastic bag and that's what we use to protect the
wound, the incision, as we take the lung out.
There is the potential and it is probably better reported in other types of tumors, but we always are concerned about the potential for seeding of the tract of the incision with
with tumor and because of that we remove the specimen with the tumor in it through a plastic bag that comes out of the chest in a protected fashion.
And you can see if we paused it.
Well, it's OK.
You can kind of see here that the vessel or the blood supply to the remaining lobe is intact there with lots of little staple lines from the branches and the bronchial staple line there as well.
And what we're doing here is to check to make sure there are no additional lymph nodes.
And we're, quote, drying it up to make sure everything's satisfactory before we finish the operation.
Eventually, I'm sorry, eventually the remaining lung will expand and take up the space of the removed lobe, and the function of the lung is going to be fine.

And maybe what we'll do right now, I think our video is about to end, and maybe what we'll do is bring in Kyla, our clinical nurse specialist, to talk about some of the things that occur after surgery.
One of the parts of our program at UW Hospital is that we actually have a separate cardiothoracic floor.
We actually no longer have an intensive care unit that's separate or different.
We have an adaptable acuity care unit where every bed can be transformed into an ICU bed or a general care bed.
It's a 27 bed unit.
And it does require a lot of training of the nurses to be able to do both.
And so we actually have two dedicated nurses that are nurse specialists, one for the cardiac side of the floor and one for the thoracic program.
And Kyler is our thoracic clinical specialist.
And so maybe we'll move away from the video now and talk to Kyla.
